title: DAX - Data Analysis Expressions
summary: DAX is the formula language for Power BI, Analysis Services, and Power Pivot. At P3, DAX mastery is a hiring bar: every consultant writes DAX daily for measures, calc groups, and RLS predicates.
content:
DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is Microsoft's formula language used in Power BI, SQL Server Analysis Services (SSAS) Tabular, and Power Pivot. It powers calculated columns, measures, calculation groups, and row-level security predicates.

P3 internal standards: prefer measures over calculated columns; use variables (VAR) for readability and performance; avoid FILTER(ALL()) when CALCULATE + simple boolean filters suffice; never use EARLIER in modern code (use variables instead).

Key references: SQLBI patterns, P3 Adaptive blog series on context transition, Marco Russo & Alberto Ferrari's Definitive Guide to DAX.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: RLS - Row-Level Security
summary: RLS in Power BI restricts data access at the row level via DAX filters bound to user identity. Used heavily at P3 for client tenant isolation in multi-tenant reports.
content:
RLS (Row-Level Security) is a Power BI feature that filters dataset rows based on the authenticated user's identity. RLS roles are defined in the model with DAX boolean predicates; users (or AAD groups) are mapped to roles in the workspace or via XMLA.

P3 patterns: dynamic RLS using USERPRINCIPALNAME() joined to a security dimension; static RLS for simple region-based scenarios; OLS (Object-Level Security) for column-level secrets. Always test RLS via the 'View as Role' feature before publishing.

Pitfall: bidirectional cross-filters can leak rows across RLS boundaries; audit relationships before assuming a model is locked down.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: OBO - On-Behalf-Of Flow
summary: OAuth2 OBO flow lets a backend exchange a user's access token for a downstream-service token, preserving user identity. P3 uses OBO heavily for LibreChat -> MCP -> Graph chains.
content:
OBO (On-Behalf-Of) is an OAuth2 grant flow defined in RFC 8693. A middle-tier service receives a user-delegated token, then exchanges it (with its own client credentials) for a token to a downstream API, scoped to the original user.

P3 use cases: LibreChat receives a user's AAD token, calls getGraphApiToken to mint a Graph-scoped OBO token, then forwards that to the MCP gateway in the X-Microsoft-Token header. The gateway uses it verbatim against Foundry IQ KB retrieve for ACL-trimmed search.

Common gotchas: OBO requires the middle-tier app to be configured with the downstream API as a 'known client'; admin consent must cover all scopes in the chain; refresh tokens are not issued in pure OBO (cache the access token instead).
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: MCP - Model Context Protocol
summary: Anthropic's open protocol for LLM tool/resource exposure. P3 runs ~10 MCP servers (M365, Salesforce, Asana, Harvest, Power BI, IQ Gateway, Sandbox, Skills, Visual, Archie).
content:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard from Anthropic for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to LLM-powered agents. Transports include stdio, SSE, and streamable-HTTP. Specification at modelcontextprotocol.io.

P3 fleet (production): p3-m365-mcp (47301), p3-salesforce-mcp (47302), p3-asana-mcp (47304), p3-harvest-mcp (47305), p3-powerbi-mcp (7010), p3-iq-mcp-gateway (47366), p3-sandbox-mcp, p3-archie-mcp (8082). All speak streamable-HTTP and integrate with LibreChat + Claude Code.

Auth patterns at P3: bridge mode (3 headers: X-Bridge-Token + X-Microsoft-Token + X-User-Email) is preferred over JWT/OBO for inter-service trust; per-user delegated tokens flow via the X-Microsoft-Token header for ACL-trimmed retrieval.
author: P3 IQ Practice

title: KQL - Kusto Query Language
summary: Microsoft's query language for log/telemetry analytics, used in Azure Data Explorer, Application Insights, Log Analytics, Sentinel, and Microsoft Fabric Real-Time Intelligence.
content:
KQL (Kusto Query Language) is a read-only query language for time-series and log data, originally built for Azure Data Explorer. Pipeline syntax (|) chains operators like where, summarize, extend, join, render.

P3 use cases: querying Application Insights for LibreChat request traces; Sentinel for security investigations; Fabric eventhouses for real-time dashboards. Pattern: always project early to reduce data volume; summarize by bin(timestamp, 1h) for time bucketing; parse for unstructured log text.

Tooling: Kusto.Explorer (desktop), ADX web UI (dataexplorer.azure.com), or via REST. Power BI has a native KQL connector for DirectQuery scenarios.
author: P3 IQ Practice
